g.data-narration: Semaphores are fundamental synchronization primitives introduced by Edsger Dijkstra in 1965 for process synchronization
A semaphore is a non-negative integer synchronization variable with two atomic operations: wait decrements the value and blocks if negative, while signal increments and wakes waiting processes
g.text: A synchronization variable - non-negative integer with two atomic operations:
Used for: Mutual Exclusion • Resource Management • Process Synchronization

g.data-narration: Semaphores prevent race conditions and are essential for concurrent programming in operating systems
Wait operation decrements the semaphore and blocks if the value becomes negative; signal increments and wakes one waiting process
g.text: A semaphore S is an integer variable with TWO atomic operations:
Atomicity is crucial - operations must be uninterruptible!

g.data-narration: Counting semaphores range from zero to N for multiple resources; binary semaphores only have values zero or one for mutual exclusion
g.text: Range: 0 to N (N = initial resource count)
Use: Control access to multiple instances

g.data-narration: The wait operation checks if semaphore is non-positive, blocks the process if negative, then decrements the value
Signal operation increments the semaphore value and wakes one waiting process from the queue if any exist
Atomic semaphore operations require hardware support through test-and-set or compare-and-swap instructions
g.text: Atomic operations require hardware support. Two common approaches:
atomically reads and writes a boolean flag
compares value, swaps only if equal

g.data-narration: Binary semaphore initialized to one provides mutual exclusion for critical sections in concurrent processes
Initialize sync equals zero to force one process to wait for another to complete before proceeding
g.text: Process A must wait for Process B to complete a task:

g.data-narration: Producer-Consumer uses three semaphores: empty equals N, full equals zero, and mutex equals one for proper synchronization
Producer waits on empty then mutex, adds to buffer, then signals mutex and full in sequence
Remember key initializations: mutex equals one, empty equals N, full equals zero, sync equals zero determines behavior
Avoid wrong initialization, forgetting to signal which causes deadlock, or using one semaphore for multiple purposes
g.text: Wrong init: binary semaphore to 0 instead of 1 for mutual exclusion
Forgetting to signal after wait leads to deadlock
Using same semaphore for both mutual exclusion and synchronization

g.data-narration: Remember P stands for Prolaag meaning try decrease, V for Verhoog meaning increase, and binary semaphores differ from mutex
g.text: wait() and signal() must be atomic

g.data-narration: Semaphores solve critical section problems and enable proper process synchronization in operating systems
g.text: Semaphores solve critical section and synchronization problems
